concept_name: Negative Time Roots in Kinematic Equations — What They Really Mean
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 6.9 — Quadratic in t; split from uniform_acceleration bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: A child throws a ball straight up from a twenty-metre-tall tower in Mumbai. The teacher asks — 'when does the ball hit the ground?' The student solves the quadratic equation and gets TWO answers: plus five seconds and minus one second. The plus five is the real answer — the ball hits the ground five seconds after the throw. The minus one tells a story about the PAST — it says that, if the ball had been thrown from the ground one second EARLIER, it would have followed this same parabolic path. The negative root is not an error; it is a mathematical ghost from the past.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A rocket fired upward from the top of a building. The quadratic gives two intersection times with ground level. The positive root is when it lands. The negative root, if it exists, is when a virtual rocket following the same equation would have been at ground level BEFORE the actual launch from the building's roof. It is a mathematical artefact, not a physical event.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A cricket ball hit from a high stage, with initial upward velocity. Its position-time equation is quadratic. Solving y equals zero gives two roots — one in the future (when it lands), one in the past (when the same motion extrapolated backward would have passed through ground level before the real shot was played).

formulas.equation_of_landing: 0 = h0 + u t - (1/2) g t^2     (ball hits ground)
formulas.two_roots: Two roots: t_plus (future, the real landing) and t_minus (past, the mathematical ghost)

constraints: Kinematic equations are exact expressions, so they yield algebraic solutions for ALL real t — including negative t
A negative t root represents 'extrapolation backward in time from the start of motion'
In physical problems, reject any t < 0 and take only t > 0
When u^2 + 2 g h0 < 0, the quadratic has no real root — the object never reaches the specified position

STATE_1.title: Two Roots From a Quadratic — Which Is Real?

scene_composition.text:
Scenario

Ball thrown UP from a 20 m tower
  initial height  h0 = 20 m
  initial velocity u = +20 m/s (UP = +)
  g = 10 m/s^2 (approx)

Question: when does the ball hit the ground?
Set y = 0 in  y = h0 + u t - (1/2) g t^2

  0 = 20 + 20 t - 5 t^2
  5 t^2 - 20 t - 20 = 0
  t^2 - 4 t - 4 = 0

Quadratic formula:
  t = (4 +- sqrt(16 + 16)) / 2
    = (4 +- sqrt(32)) / 2
    = (4 +- 5.66) / 2

  t_plus  =  4.83 s
  t_minus = -0.83 s

Two answers!

+4.83 s is easy to understand —
it is when the ball actually lands.

-0.83 s is mysterious — negative time?
  Did the ball travel backward in time?
  Is this a calculation error?

(Neither. It's a ghost from the past
of the MATHEMATICAL extension of the
same physics. Real-world physics says:
reject the negative root.)
Two Roots From a Quadratic — Which Is Real?

tts_sentences.text_en: A ball is thrown straight up from a twenty-metre tall tower with initial speed twenty metres per second. When does it hit the ground?
Choose up as positive. Write y equals h-zero plus u t minus half g t squared, and set y to zero for the ground.
That gives a quadratic. t squared minus four t minus four equals zero.
Apply the quadratic formula. You get two solutions — t equals four point eight three seconds, and t equals minus zero point eight three seconds.
The positive one is easy — that is when the ball lands. But what does the negative root mean? Did the ball travel back in time? Is the calculation wrong?
Neither. The negative root is a real mathematical solution with a specific physical interpretation. The next two states unpack it so that you understand WHY to reject it in a physics problem.

STATE_2.title: The Ghost of the Past — Extrapolating the Trajectory Backward

scene_composition.text:
Timeline

  t = -0.83 s   ground level (mathematical)
       |
       v
  [virtual ball rising from ground]
       |
       v
  t = 0         ball leaves the tower at h = 20 m
       |        with v = 20 m/s upward
       v
  [ball rises, peaks, falls]
       |
       v
  t = 4.83 s    ball hits ground (real)

At t = -0.83 the equation says y = 0.
But the ball was NOT at ground then --
it was waiting in the thrower's hand
at the top of the tower.

Physical interpretation

The kinematic equation assumes a
single projectile with fixed u and g,
valid for ALL t.

If we had flown the SAME equation
backward from t = 0, the ball would
have been at ground level 0.83 s
before the real throw.

BUT the ball was NOT actually there
at that time. The equation describes
a mathematical extension of the real
motion.

Reject any t < 0 for physical
problems that start at t = 0.
The Ghost of the Past — Extrapolating the Trajectory Backward

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is what the negative root actually means. The kinematic equation takes the single motion — same u, same g — and extends it for ALL times, both positive and negative.
If we run the formula backward from t equals zero, we find that the same motion extended into the past would have been at ground level zero point eight three seconds BEFORE the real throw.
But in reality the ball was not anywhere near the ground then. It was in the student's hand at the top of the tower, waiting to be thrown. The real motion started at t equals zero.
So the negative root is a mathematical ghost. It describes a hypothetical continuation of the motion backward in time — not a physical event.
For any problem that defines motion starting at t equals zero, reject every negative t root. Only positive roots describe what actually happened.

This interpretation is important on JEE — the paper may give you a quadratic and ask for both roots, then ask you to identify the physical one. Knowing why the negative is rejected is the key to writing the correct physics justification.

STATE_3.title: Three Cases — When Both, One, or Neither Root Is Physical

scene_composition.text:
Three cases

Case 1  Both roots positive
  Happens when ball, starting positive,
  passes through y = 0 TWICE in its real
  flight (impossible for ball thrown up
  from a tower; happens for a ball thrown
  horizontally that rises and falls to 0 twice).
  Both are physical.

Case 2  One positive, one negative
  Ball thrown up from a tower at h0 > 0.
  Positive root = landing time.
  Negative root = mathematical ghost before
   motion began. Reject.

Case 3  Both negative (rare)
  Motion would have crossed y = 0 only in the
  past. No physical landing occurs in t > 0.
  In such a problem, revisit your sign conventions.
Three Cases — When Both, One, or Neither Root Is Physical
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Three possible combinations of roots for a kinematic quadratic in t.

Case one — both roots positive. This happens, for example, if a ball is thrown with enough upward velocity from below the ground level (say, from inside a well) and crosses ground level twice as it rises and then falls. Both times are physical.
Case two — one positive, one negative. Thrown from a tower or platform, lands at the positive root, and the negative root is the mathematical ghost before the motion began. Reject the negative.

Case three — both roots negative. The motion, extrapolated backward, would have crossed the target level only in the past. No physical landing occurs in t greater than zero. In such problems, usually, the sign convention was set wrong or the object was never going to reach the target.

When you see two roots, always check their signs. Always reject t less than zero for problems that start at t equals zero. This single check keeps you from claiming 'the ball landed a second before it was thrown'.

epic_c_branches.misconception: A negative time root is a calculation mistake and should be ignored without understanding
trigger_phrases: I got negative t is it an error
did I mess up the quadratic

STATE_1.title: Your Belief — Negative t Means I Did the Math Wrong

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

I solved 0 = 20 + 20 t - 5 t^2
and got t = -0.83, 4.83.

Negative time is nonsensical in
physics. So I must have made a
sign error somewhere.

Let me redo the quadratic...

(No — the math is correct.
The -0.83 IS a real algebraic
solution. Understand what it is.)
Your Belief — Negative t Means I Did the Math Wrong
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: A natural first reaction to a negative t — it must be a calculation mistake, so go back and redo the algebra.
In most cases, the math is correct. Kinematic equations for projectiles often give one positive and one negative root. That is expected, not a bug.
Understanding what the negative root means is more useful than re-doing the algebra a third time.

STATE_2.title: Negative t Is a Real Solution — Just Not THIS Problem's Answer

scene_composition.text:
Check the math
  0 = 20 + 20 t - 5 t^2
  5 t^2 - 20 t - 20 = 0
  t^2 - 4 t - 4 = 0
  t = (4 +- sqrt(32)) / 2
  t = 4.83 or -0.83

Both roots satisfy the equation.
No algebra mistake.

Interpretation
  The EQUATION is a model that assumes
  the motion has been going for all time
  with the same u and g.
  Negative t root = when THAT model
  says ball would be at y = 0 in the past.

In OUR problem, motion starts at t = 0.
So only t > 0 is physical.

Why we do not discard the math:
it is correct for the MODEL.
Negative t Is a Real Solution — Just Not THIS Problem's Answer
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Check the math first. Five t squared minus twenty t minus twenty equals zero. Quadratic formula gives t equals four point eight three or minus zero point eight three. Both roots exactly satisfy the equation.
No algebra mistake. The negative root is a mathematically valid solution.
The key insight is that the kinematic EQUATION assumes the same u and g for ALL t — past and future. The equation does not know that your problem started at t equals zero.
So the negative root says — if you extrapolate the same motion backward, it would have been at y equals zero zero point eight three seconds before the real throw.

In YOUR physical problem, motion starts at t equals zero. The negative root describes a hypothetical before that start. Reject it. But don't doubt the math — the math is right. Only the physical interpretation filters out the negative root.

STATE_3.title: The Rule — Algebraic vs Physical Roots

scene_composition.text:
Rule for every quadratic in kinematics

Step 1  Solve the quadratic.
         Keep ALL real roots.

Step 2  For each root, check
         if it is physical.
           - t >= 0 (for problems starting at t = 0)
           - consistent with the scenario
             (e.g. does motion actually occur then)

Step 3  Discard any root that fails
         the physical check.

Step 4  The remaining root is your
         answer.

Exam tip
  JEE often asks 'interpret the
  other root' as a follow-up. Be
  ready to say 'mathematical
  extension, not physical'.
The Rule — Algebraic vs Physical Roots
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the rule. Solve the quadratic, keep all real roots, then filter.
For each root, ask — is it physically meaningful? For problems starting at t equals zero, require t at least zero. For other problems, check against the scenario.
Discard any root that fails the physical check. Keep the one that remains.

JEE sometimes asks a follow-up question — 'interpret the other root'. Be ready with the answer: it is a mathematical extension of the same motion to a time when the physical motion was not actually happening. Not a physical event.

regeneration_variants.label: Downward throw
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Ball thrown DOWN from 20 m with speed 10 m/s. 0 = 20 - 10 t - 5 t^2. t = (-10 +- sqrt(100 + 400))/(-10) = (-10 +- 22.36)/(-10). t_plus = -1.24 s (past, reject), t_minus = 3.24 s (future, physical).

regeneration_variants.label: Discriminant negative — no real root
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Projectile at 5 m/s upward trying to reach height 100 m. 100 = 5 t - 5 t^2. discriminant 25 - 2000 < 0. No real roots. Ball never reaches 100 m.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: When discriminant < 0, the scenario is physically impossible for the given parameters
